data.title: The S&P 500 Clocks A New Record High As Geopolitics Hold Index Back From Rising Higher
data.description:
The second-longest ever winning streak for the S&P 500 (SPX) was broken on Monday, 20 April 2026. The streak had endured for 13 days, one short of the all-time record.

While the streak was broken, all was not bad for Wall Street bulls, who could celebrate the index hitting a new all-time record high closing value of 7,165.08 on Friday, 24 April 2026.

The streak largely came about as a result of the ceasefire in the Iran War, which was at risk of ending before President Trump announced an extension during the past week. The market's new highs came in response to that event, the prospect of diplomatic talks between the U.S. and Iran soon resuming, and the strength of earnings out of the market's technology sector.

All these things kept the trajectory of the S&P 500 running along the bottom end of the redzone forecast range we added to the alternative futures chart back in February, as shown in its latest update. That range assumes investors would be focused on the current quarter of 2026-Q2, which they certainly have been, even with the disruption of the Iran war geopolitical event.

The centerline of the redzone forecast range provides a reasonable estimate of where the S&P 500 would have gone in the absence of the Iran war. From Friday, 17 April 2026, through Friday, 24 April 2026, the index has ranged between 2.5 and 3.0% below that trajectory, which is to say the geopolitical event is keeping the S&P 500 from rising even higher.

What happens next for the S&P 500 will be determined by investor reactions to the random onset of new information. Here are the market-moving headlines that affected the trajectory of the index during the week that was.

Monday, 20 April 2026

  • Signs and portents for the U.S. economy:
    • Oil prices rise anew after a US-Iran standoff in the Strait of Hormuz strands tankers
  • Future chief Fed official goes up for Senate interview:
    • Fed chief nominee Warsh commits to central bank's independence, with limits
      • Fed chair nominee Warsh has big ideas. Here are some, in his own words
  • Bigger trouble developing in China:
    • China curtailing, not banning fuel exports, shipping data shows
  • BOJ officials expected to pass on hiking rates in April, but look forward to June 2026:
    • Exclusive: BOJ is likely to hold off raising rates in April, sources say
      • Japan households' inflation expectations steady, BOJ April hike bets fade
    • BOJ's hawkish hints keep rate hike on the cards
  • ECB officials thinking about getting more data before ditching their perfect monetary policy:
    • ECB needs more data before firm policy conclusions, Lagarde says
  • Wall Street closes slightly down on renewed tensions between US, Iran
    • Wall Street ends down as the U.S.-Iran peace deal remains uncertain

Tuesday, 21 April 2026

  • Signs and portents for the U.S. economy:
    • Oil prices climb 5% after Trump says he does not want to extend Iran ceasefire
      • US crude and fuel exports surge to record highs, but it's not enough
      • Record surge in gasoline receipts boosts US retail sales, but weakness is looming
      • Price hikes, outlook cuts - What airlines are doing as fuel costs surge
    • US pending home sales beat expectations in March
  • Prospective new chief official calls for 'regime change' at Fed, current Fed officials weigh in with suggestions:
    • Warsh calls for 'regime change' at Fed, new inflation approach
      • As Warsh faces hearing, a framework for smaller Fed balance sheet emerges
      • Five things we learned at Fed nominee Warsh's Senate hearing
    • Fed's Waller calls for overhaul of regional Fed bank operations
      • Fed's Waller would 'absolutely' oppose firing reserve bank presidents over rate disagreement
  • Bigger trouble, stimulus developing in China:
    • China's youth jobless rate rises to 16.9% in March
    • China targets 100 trillion yuan services sector by 2030 in upgrade push
  • Bigger trouble developing in Eurozone:
    • Germany's big carmakers used to lead the race in China, but now they're 'for the parents'
  • ECB officials priming to leave Eurozone interest rates unchanged, think private credit is a threat:
    • ECB must keep cool head on rates amid Iran war, de Guindos says
    • ECB sees private credit as risk to financial stability, de Guindos says
  • Wall Street extends losses as a U.S.-Iran resolution remains uncertain

Wednesday, 22 April 2026

  • Signs and portents for the U.S. economy:
    • Trump extends Iran ceasefire, maintains Hormuz blockade
      • Oil prices rise after reports container ships in Hormuz hit by gunfire
      • How the Iran war oil and gas supply shock compares with past disruptions
  • Fed officials expected to cut U.S. interest rates before end of 2026:
    • Fed rate cut pushed back to late 2026 on war-related inflation risks
  • Bigger stimulus developing in China:
    • China unveils plan to make cities more youth, child friendly
  • BOJ officials worried about Iran war dragging out:
    • BOJ warns prolonged Mideast tensions could raise costs, corporate defaults
  • S&P 500, Nasdaq end at record as Trump extends ceasefire deadline with Iran
    • S&P, Nasdaq close at records on Iran ceasefire extension, earnings
      • S&P 500 PE looks cheap, but high oil prices still pose a threat

Thursday, 23 April 2026

  • Signs and portents for the U.S. economy:
    • Brent crude futures jump on Trump's threats over Strait of Hormuz
      • Trump on gas prices: Americans should expect to pay more 'for a little while'
      • Record demand can't save US airlines from Iran war fuel shock
        • American Airlines dims 2026 forecast as high fuel costs hurt margins
        • Trump administration nears $500 million Spirit rescue as Iran fuel shock hits airlines
    • US fixed 30-year mortgage rate drops to 6.23%
    • New grads are finding jobs faster despite a competitive job market, says report
  • Fed officials thinking about what they mean when they say they have a 2% inflation target, maybe cutting rates in late 2026:
    • A Warsh-led Fed's 2% inflation goal might be a different 2%
    • Fed rate cut pushed back to late 2026 on war-related inflation risks: Reuters poll
  • ECB officials expected to ditch their perfect monetary policy with rate hikes in June 2026:
    • ECB to raise rates in June on war-driven inflation but path beyond unclear
  • Meanwhile, bigger trouble developing in the Eurozone:
    • Euro zone business activity contracted in April as costs rocketed, PMI shows
  • U.S. stocks end lower as conflict in the Strait of Hormuz intensifies
    • Stocks close lower on fading hopes for quick Iran deal, mixed quarterly earnings

Friday, 24 April 2026

  • Signs and portents for the U.S. economy:
    • Oil prices fall on report Iran foreign minister to arrive in Pakistan for peace talks
    • 20,000 job cuts at Meta, Microsoft raise concern that AI-driven labor crisis is here
  • Incoming chief Fed official wants better inflation data, has path to secure Senate confirmation cleared as DOJ bends to Senator Tillis' demand to end criminal probe of Fed:
    • Warsh's quest for better inflation data is a well-worn, difficult pursuit for Fed
    • DOJ drops criminal probe of Fed Chair Powell, removes hurdle for Warsh confirmation
      • Tillis Praises Warsh but Says He Won’t Back Him for Fed Chair
  • Pervasive AI technologies and bigger stimulus developing in China:
    • DeepSeek previews new AI model adapted to run on Huawei chips
      • After call from Beijing, China's auto industry races to embed AI in just about everything
      • China to curb US investment in tech companies, Bloomberg News reports
      • White House accuses China of industrial-scale theft of AI technology
    • China first-quarter fiscal spending quickens as Beijing seeks to spur growth
  • BOJ officials get reason to not hike rate this month:
    • Japan's core inflation stays below BOJ target, energy risks grow
  • S&P 500, Nasdaq snatch another record as U.S. continues ceasefire negotiations with Iran
    • S&P 500, Nasdaq close at records on tech lift, Iran peace talk hopes
    • US chipmakers hit record highs as Intel turbocharges AI rally
      • Intel forecasts second-quarter revenue above estimates, shares jump 19%
    • Most US companies seen sticking with quarterly reporting
    • More than 25% chance the S&P 500 will hit 8,000 in 2026 – Kalshi

The CME Group's FedWatch Tool continued to anticipate no Federal Reserve rate cuts in 2026, though with a growing bias toward a quarter-point rate cut over time.

The Atlanta Fed's GDPNow tool forecast of real GDP growth in 2026-Q1 ticked down to +1.2%, dipping from its projection of +1.3% growth a week earlier.

data.title: Allegion: An Undervalued Stock Catering To The Basic Need Of Security
data.description:
Allegion plc (ALLE) is an American (but Irish-domiciled) provider of security products for homes and businesses globally. Founded in 1908, but spun out into an independent company from Ingersoll Rand Inc. (IR) in 2014, Allegion is now a $12 billion (by market cap) security competitor that employs over 13,000 people. The company reports results across two geographic segments: Allegion Americas, 79% of FY 2025 revenue; and Allegion International, 21%.

Revenue is roughly split 50/50 between new construction and aftermarket. The company produces and sells a number of security-related products and accessories, including: access control systems, door closers, exit devices, electronic security products, locks, and key systems. These products are sold under a range of leading brands, including: CISA, Interflex, LCN, Schlage, SimonsVoss, and Von Duprin.

Allegion is a fantastic business because it’s more than the sum of its parts. It doesn’t simply sell products; it sells security itself. And security is critically important to one’s wellbeing. Based on Maslow’s Hierarchy of Needs, safety is eclipsed only by basic survival needs (such as food and water). I’d argue that crime and danger are perceived (rightly or wrongly) to be more prevalent now than in the past – a perception not helped by widespread social issues such as homelessness and drug addiction.

This perception creates elevated structural demand for products and services designed to improve safety. Furthermore, that structural demand is supercharged by technology, as high-tech security solutions (such as electronic locking systems) are more advanced, effective, and (you guessed it) expensive than analog alternatives.

That’s great news for Allegion, which supplies the global market with high-tech safety solutions designed to make lives, buildings, and valuables more secure. Due to the harsh reality we live in, which is afflicted by human nature, it’s hard to imagine any future in which safety becomes less paramount or demand for security products and services goes down, which is why Allegion is set up so well to continue increasing its revenue, profit, and dividend for many years to come.

Dividend Growth, Growth Rate, Payout Ratio and Yield

Indeed, Allegion has increased its dividend for 13 consecutive years. The 10-year dividend growth rate is 17.7%, which is very strong, but more recent dividend raises have been in a high-single-digit range (around 8% or so) – still strong, but not quite as forceful.

Shareholders get to pair that dividend growth with the market-beating 1.5% yield, which is 20 basis points higher than its own five-year average. And with the payout ratio sitting at just 30%, Allegion’s dividend appears to be ultra safe and poised for much more growth. Pairing this yield with a HSD-LDD dividend growth profile gets one to a 10%+ annualized total return pretty quickly – all on a business that’s easy to understand with secular growth drivers. That strikes me as pretty appealing.

Revenue and Earnings Growth

As much as it may be, though, a lot of the appeal is built on data from the past. However, investors must always be thinking about the data that’s likely to come in the future, as the capital of today is risked for the rewards of tomorrow. Thus, I’ll now build out a forward-looking growth trajectory for the business, which will come in handy during the valuation process.

I’ll first show you what the business has done over the last ten years in terms of its top-line and bottom-line growth. And I’ll then reveal a professional prognostication for near-term profit growth. Amalgamating the proven past with a future forecast in this way should allow us to develop an idea of where the business could be going from here.

Allegion advanced its revenue from $2.2 billion in FY 2016 to $4.1 billion in FY 2025. That’s a compound annual growth rate of 7.2%.Great top-line growth out of a large, mature company. Meantime, earnings per share grew from $2.36 to $7.44 over this period, which is a CAGR of 13.6%. Excellent. A combination of steady buybacks and expanding margins (from higher-margin tech-driven security solutions) helped to drive excess bottom-line growth.

Regarding the buybacks, Allegion reduced its outstanding share count by about 10% over this period. Looking forward, CFRA is calling for Allegion to compound its EPS at an annual rate of 9% over the next three years. While this wouldn’t be the 13%+ that Allegion did over the last decade, it’d still be a great result.

CFRA believes that Allegion has multiple bottom-line growth levers to pull, including buybacks, tech-driven security upgrades across markets, data center growth (which is more new construction on buildings that must be highly secure), and strategic acquisitions. For its part, Allegion is guiding for $8.10 in midpoint EPS for FY 2026, which would represent roughly 9% YOY growth – right in line with where CFRA is at.

If we take this forecast as a base assumption, that sets the dividend up for like-or-better (by virtue of the low payout ratio, which can be easily expanded) growth over the next decade or so. That translates to more HSD-LDD dividend growth, circling back around to the earlier mention of a straightforward 10%+ annualized total return on a great business. Hard to dislike that.

By the way, I think Warren Buffett’s Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (BRK.B) also found it hard to dislike, as the firm took a stake in Allegion during Q2 2025 – back when the stock largely had pricing similar to where it’s at now (after a 20% decline that started in the fall of 2025). Being able to buy in at a similar level and invest alongside one of the world’s great investors is a compelling notion.

Financial Position

Moving over to the balance sheet, Allegion has a good financial position. Its long-term debt/equity ratio is 1, while the interest coverage ratio is nearly 9. Allegion also has investment-grade credit ratings: BBB, Fitch; BBB, S&P. While this isn’t a bad balance sheet, I do view it as the weakest part of the business (if only because the rest of Allegion is so great).

Profitability is outstanding. Return on equity has averaged 48.2% over the last five years, while net margin has averaged 15.5%. ROE gets a boost from the balance sheet structure, but even ROIC is routinely north of 20%. A hallmark of a wonderful business is high returns on capital, and Allegion is generating very high returns on capital.

Overall, I see so much to like about Allegion as a long-term investment. And with economies of scale, brand recognition, a built-out distribution network, and a large installed base, which leads to “stickiness” and recurring revenue, the company does benefit from durable competitive advantages.

Of course, there are risks to consider. Litigation, regulation, and competition are omnipresent risks in every industry. Competition, in particular, is an issue, as this space is filled with very competent and fierce competitors. Around half of the company’s revenue is tied to new construction, exposing the company to the issues therein (e.g., interest rates, macroeconomics, new construction demand, labor shortages, etc.).

Being an international company, Allegion faces geopolitics, supply chain concerns, and currency exchange rates, but the company’s US lean mitigates much of this. The industry is increasingly moving toward digital/automated solutions, which means Allegion must continually stay ahead of the tech curve in order to not get left behind.

Allegion is a large, established, mature company in its space, meaning its growth may not shine relative to smaller competitors. I do see some risks to be aware of, but Allegion’s quality and growth profile need to be considered. Also, the stock’s recent 20% pullback, which has created what I think is an attractive valuation, also needs to be considered…

Valuation

The P/E ratio has dropped to 19.5. While not super low in absolute terms in isolation, this is a terrific company that typically commands suitable multiples. To that point, the stock’s five-year average P/E ratio is 21.3, so we’re decently below that level right now. The P/CF ratio of 15.6, which is not egregious at all, is also well below its own five-year average of 18.4. And the yield, as noted earlier, is higher than its own recent historical average.

So the stock looks cheap when looking at basic valuation metrics. But how cheap might it be? What would a rational estimate of intrinsic value look like?

I valued shares using a two-stage dividend discount model analysis. I factored in a 10% discount rate, a 10-year dividend growth rate of 13%, and a long-term dividend growth rate of 8%. The near-term dividend growth rate I’m assuming shouldn’t be a high hurdle to clear for a company that grew its EPS at 13%+ and its dividend at 17%+ over the last decade.

I’m building in a slowdown here, reflective of recent dividend growth deceleration and the three-year 9% EPS forecast. With the payout ratio being as low as it is, a 13% dividend growth rate would not be challenging or unrealistic against 9% EPS growth. I’m then expecting a natural slowdown in the future as the company continues to mature. The DDM analysis gives me a fair value of $181.07. The reason I use a dividend discount model analysis is because a business is ultimately equal to the sum of all the future cash flow it can provide.

The DDM analysis is a tailored version of the discounted cash flow model analysis, as it simply substitutes dividends and dividend growth for cash flow and growth. It then discounts those future dividends back to the present day to account for the time value of money, since a dollar tomorrow is not worth the same amount as a dollar today. I find it to be a fairly accurate way to value dividend growth stocks. To me, this stock looks cheaper than it ought to be.

But we’ll now compare that valuation with where two professional stock analysis firms have come out at. This adds balance, depth, and perspective to our conclusion.

Morningstar, a leading and well-respected stock analysis firm, rates stocks on a 5-star system. 1 star would mean a stock is substantially overvalued; 5 stars would mean a stock is substantially undervalued. 3 stars would indicate roughly fair value. Morningstar rates ALLE as a 4-star stock, with a fair value estimate of $170.00.

CFRA is another professional analysis firm, and I like to compare my valuation opinion to theirs to see if I’m out of line. They similarly rate stocks on a 1-5 star scale, with 1 star meaning a stock is a strong sell and 5 stars meaning a stock is a strong buy. 3 stars is a hold. CFRA rates ALLE as a 5-star “STRONG BUY”, with a 12-month target price of $212.00.

I came out roughly in the middle. Averaging the three numbers out gives us a final valuation of $187.69, which would indicate the stock is possibly 23% undervalued.

Bottom line: Allegion is a high-quality company catering to the basic need of security. Secular growth is supercharged by an ongoing shift to higher-margin, high-tech solutions. With a market-beating yield, double-digit dividend growth, a low payout ratio, nearly 15 consecutive years of dividend increases, and the potential that shares are 23% undervalued, this looks like a fantastic opportunity to invest in a world-class business alongside Warren Buffett.

Note from D&I: How safe is ALLE's dividend? We ran the stock through Simply Safe Dividends, and as we go to press, its Dividend Safety Score is 61. Dividend Safety Scores range from 0 to 100. A score of 50 is average, 75 or higher is excellent, and 25 or lower is weak. With this in mind, ALLE’s dividend appears Safe with an unlikely risk of being cut.

Disclosure: I’m long ALLE.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Why This May Be A Particularly Good Moment To Buy I Bonds
data.description:
As I write this there is only one unknown factor for the upcoming reset of I Bonds and that factor will be resolved at the May 1 reset. That's the fixed rate, the rate for real return above the inflation rate, which is currently .9% and very likely to remain at that .9% when the long term rate is announced on May 1. Though the rate itself doesn't seem large, it is in fact very important as it will remain as the fixed rate for the life of the I Bond is potentially as far as 30 years into the future. The fixed rate has been calculated for many years by taking 65% of the real rate of return of 5-year TIPS over the past six months. With the reset coming in five days it would take a drastic change for the fixed rate to be announced as anything other than the current .9%.

What we know with absolute certainty is that the variable rate was fully established as soon as the March inflation rate was published. In the last months of 2025 inflation had appeared to be subsiding. This trend continued into February. With the beginning of the war in Iran, however, the inflation rate began to jump sharply and it is a reasonable guess that it might continue to do so for a period of time as long as the war goes on.

If we assume the fixed rate holds at 0.90% with the variable rate rising to 3.34%, the composite rate (fixed plus variable) from May through October of this year adds up to 4.26%. With a composite rate of 4.03%, for I Bonds purchased from October 2025 through April 30 2026. The overall composite rate for the full year going forward sums to an overall return of 4.16%. Note that this return is in the same general area as the return on the 10-year Treasury Note and much higher than the rate on short term T Bills where many investors park short term cash.

In previous articles I have written about the unusual advantage an I Bond investor has in the days before the May 1 or November 1 reset. A favorable quirk in the TreasuryDirect I Bond rules is that I Bonds purchased at any time of the month will receive interest for the full month. This adds to the appeal of I Bonds bought in the next day or two as it adds a little extra in the immediate return if you buy just before the reset on May 1 or November 1. Your one year return consists of the return for the past six months along with the return set in stone for the next six months. Think about it. It's as if you were able to buy a stock or an index at its price a full year in the past so that the one year return is guaranteed. Who could say no to that advantage? If you don't like that return, you do nothing. If you like it, you pile in and buy as much as the allowable $10,000 and possibly more using the Gift Box.

Every Technical Detail Favors The Buyer - Including The Gift Box

Of course bond returns are minuscule compared to stock returns, but if you want to own any super-safe bonds, the current I Bond is a good option. It's particularly good as fixed rates are likely to change on November 1 in an unfavorable direction. Fixed income yields in general are more likely than not to fall as the Federal Reserve cuts rates at some point this year and possibly continues cutting well into next year. While it is always possible that rates at the longer end of the yield curve stay as they are or even go up a bit, but it seems likely that rates on straight (non inflation adjusted) Treasury Notes up to 5 years will go down. There is consequently a likelihood that other sorts of fixed income such as CDs will follow. The fixed rate at the next reset will also be likely to fall somewhat as it is, remember, based upon the real return of the 5 year TIPS.

If you want to play this, you should buy your desired quantity of I Bonds up to $10,000 as soon as possible, ideally early next week, so as not to get bumped beyond May 1. If you want to buy in a big way, you should take a last opportunity to make a pair with trusted friends or family members and buy on a larger scale using the Gift Box. I have done this in the past and might do it again this year if I find an interested partner. As with your own purchase you should do it well in advance of May 1, ideally by April 28, next Tuesday, to be sure the purchase is accepted before midnight April 30. Like all I Bond purchases Gift Box returns begin on the first day of the month in which the I Bond is purchased. So if you want to add a Gift Box I Bond, buy now or be prepared to kick yourself later as all the established details are now in your favor. I intend to buy a $10K I Bond today or tomorrow.

There will likely be major changes in the Gift Box sooner or later and TreasuryDirect is "requesting" that Gift Box participants have their Gift Box I Bonds "delivered" ASAP. I myself and all Gift Box participants received a message from TreasuryDirect with this request, starting with the line "Treasury is always looking for ways to enhance your experience." That may well be true but the phone answerers at TreasuryDirect have a history of being somewhat disorganized in the face of major changes. This was especially so during the recent temporary but steep rise in inflation from 2021 through 2023, peaking at 9.1% in the middle of 2022. TreasuryDirect was caught off guard both in their ability to fill purchases in a timely fashion and in having not originally thought through the Gift Box and what impact major events might have on it. As a result they must "suggest" actions that they know they can't really enforce. I almost never respond to government "suggestions" and have no plans to respond to this one. For now one can use the Gift Box as before.

The Likelihood Of Extreme Volatility Plays To The Strength Of I Bonds

At the present moment most analysts who bother to think about these things seem to feel that inflation will likely remain about the same as the present all the way out to the distant event horizon. Your response to that perceived fact will depend upon your point of view. I'll be long gone in 30 years (being 81) but my daughter who is the designated heir for my I Bonds will likely be in her early 80s. I have explained to her how I Bonds work and given her the cash to buy an I Bond on her own, a task that she performed successfully. A lot can happen in 30 years, especially when it comes to inflation. If your need is "inflation insurance," you would do well to consider the amount of U.S. government debt which is likely to be engaged by some form of money printing which will bring with it major inflation. I don't actually see my I Bond portfolio as insurance that you can cash and take to the grocery store, but more as an asset class you will be happy to have as part of your total portfolio if major inflation takes place. I have purchased I Bonds regularly since the year 2000 and they have performed spectacularly to that goal.

The present moment has more than the normal degree of uncertainty. President Trump's policies could produce anything from inflation to deflation, maybe one after the other, and I Bonds have a wonderful quality talked about in the final section below. They are structured to protect you against both inflation and deflation. We may in fact get both within a year or two. A new Federal Reserve Chair, Kevin Warsh, is likely to win an easy vote within a day or two. He already has potentially incompatible goals - lowering interest rates and reducing the Fed's large deficit derived from years of supporting large issuance of long term bonds. There are also wild-card factors. There is a risk That the war in Iran keeps oil prices high for longer than many observers expect which would lead to higher and longer term inflation.

Two other factors are wild cards which could go either way - toward inflation or deflation. There is weak consumer sentiment despite decent growth in employment. The confluence of these factors is unusual and it is possible that a weaker economy is on the way with a lead time. A major recession could pull down price changes into negative territory. I Bonds are specifically set up to deal with that as the variable rate never goes negative. Further tariffs could also go either way. The largest unknown factor is AI, which might have a positive impact on corporate costs and increase profits but might on the other hand result in layoffs in large numbers. One recent example is Amazon (AMZN) which has laid off numbers by the tens of thousands in their warehouses. No one without a crystal ball knows the answer but I Bonds work better than any other Treasury Bonds to defend against either negative result.

For what it's worth the 100-plus years of good records show that inflation has a very long term average of 3% although that 3% average puts together a lumpy and bumpy history running from the extreme deflation of the 1930s to the extreme inflation of the 1970s which I myself experienced. I will continue to buy I Bonds as long as they are offered. The ones I was fortunate to buy starting in 2000 have amounted to a sum I won't boast about. I actually bought them casually, almost on a whim at a time when the stock market looked awful and overpriced (around 2000-2001) and soon crashed spectacularly. So buy I Bonds by early next week if they meet your personal goals.

In writing about I Bonds I assume that the majority of my readers are already well informed about the way they work. On the other hand, a few will be new to I Bonds so I always include some form of the following section giving a sort of beginner's version on how I Bonds work and how they resemble and differ from similar assets. Even if you have known about I Bonds for a long time you may want to refresh your memory in case you have forgotten some details. I do so myself whenever writing a new article on I Bonds.

What Are I Bonds And How Do They Work?

I Bonds are a form of US Treasury Savings Bond which is inflation-adjusted. Like ordinary EE Savings Bonds, I Bonds are backed by the United States Treasury. Savings bonds have been around since 1935. Under a temporary name change to War Bonds, US Savings Bonds helped fund World War II. I Bonds have been around since 1998.

I Bonds produce returns with two components. The first is a fixed rate which provides the "real" return - real meaning inflation adjusted. For new purchases, this rate resets semiannually on May 1 and Nov. 1, or the next business day if the first day of the month happens to fall on a holiday. Once you purchase an I Bond, the real rate in force at that time continues for 30 years until the bond matures or until the owner chooses to redeem it. The second component of I Bond return is the inflation rate as represented by the Urban Consumer Price Index (CPI-U), the same index that is used for Social Security resets. The inflation rate used for I Bonds is reset semiannually at the same time the fixed rate is reset, on May 1 and November 1, and the accrued value of your I Bond is updated so that the return compounds semiannually. Note that the fixed rate and the variable rate are reset every six months, the variable rate from the CPI report, the fixed rate at the whim of the Treasury which has recently been thought to track the real return of five-year Treasury TIPS. This could change at any time but hasn't in the past six years.

You can currently buy up to $10,000 annually of electronic I Bonds in a TreasuryDirect account - that's $10,000 per Social Security Number so that you can buy the same amount for your spouse and each of your children. In the past, also you could also request that up to $5000 of your Federal income tax refund be received in the form of a paper I Bond which will be mailed to you whenever the IRS gets around to sending it. That possibility was phased out, however, on January 1, 2025.

Like all Treasury Bonds, I Bonds are not taxable by states and municipalities. Another advantage of I Bonds is that you can choose to defer paying Federal taxes until maturity or redemption. This can be helpful if you or your heir (whom you designate with each purchase) expect to have lower taxable income at some point in the future. Under some circumstances, I Bonds are tax-free when used for educational purposes - read the fine print, though.

One of the most important but seldom noticed qualities of I Bonds is that they're not only an excellent inflation hedge but a hedge against deflation as well. The reason is that the inflation component of the composite return never dips below zero. In the event of a deflationary period, the accrued inflation value of the I Bond would simply freeze at its highest level. When inflation returns, as it always has, the total value of the account begins to compound again from the highest level it has reached.

I Bonds are probably the most flexible investment available. This is because their duration is any date you choose, from one year to their maturity date of 30 years. You cannot redeem your I Bond during the first year, but after that, you choose any moment you wish if for any reason you want to get out. You can thus use them as a place to park cash for the short to intermediate term, or leave them in place and continue to have an assured long-term hedge against inflation.

I Bonds can only be purchased at TreasuryDirect where it is reasonably easy to establish an account. They remain at TreasuryDirect and do not trade in any market.

For the first five years, the price paid for redeeming a bond is giving up one quarter (three months) of returns. After five years of owning an I Bond, the forfeiting of one quarter's returns no longer applies. There are, by the way, no commissions or other costs involved in buying or selling I Bonds. I bonds can be bought at price intervals from $50 to $10,000 while electronic bonds may be bought in penny increments starting at $25. You could, for example, buy an I Bond with a face value $25.23. The Treasury Direct site has a slightly more detailed description which I also recommend that you read. If you have questions I will do my best to respond in comments.

data.title: Sirius XM: iHeartMedia Deal Could Put The Cash Flow Thesis At Risk
data.description:
The Sirius XM Holdings Inc. (SIRI) investment thesis today seems attractive to me for the following reasons, not necessarily in that order: (1) it is an extremely resilient satellite radio and streaming subscription model with low relative churn and moderate pricing power; (2) a capital return machine with high and predictable cash generation, high dividend yield, and meaningful buybacks; (3) it has in-car dominance as an embedded option with OEM integration; and (4) treated as a declining business with conservative valuation multiples.

In my article written more than a year ago about the company, I highlighted my bullish stance anchored in the idea that it is a business that fits the investment style of the Oracle of Omaha, Warren Buffett:

“The New York City-based company has healthy operating margins and a good track record of generating free cash flow, making the company attractive from a value point of view, providing high returns on its capital-notwithstanding, attracting none other than Warren Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B) as its main shareholder.”

The most recent headline involving Sirius XM is about a possible purchase of iHeartMedia, Inc. (IHRT), still in the preliminary stages, currently valued at $573 million. It also involves the participation of music industry mogul Irving Azoff and Apollo Global Management behind the scenes, potentially facilitating a deal between the parties.

As SIRI's thesis today is about FCF growth, stability, and shareholder returns, depending on the terms of the M&A, Sirius XM's capital allocation would likely shift in a less shareholder-friendly direction, and also the risk perception of the company's balance sheet—considering that iHeartMedia has considerable leverage. However, it would offer an interesting addition of cash flows and some growth optionality through synergies that SIRI's thesis currently lacks.

In this article, I will seek to go deeper into the details of recent headlines, as well as the timing of Sirius XM's fundamentals and what an M&A with iHeartMedia might look like for the broader thesis around SIRI.

SIRI: A “Melting Ice Cube” or a Cash Flow Machine?

Sirius XM is a company that has been viewed with widespread market skepticism, as sell-side consensus remains highly divided on SIRI. I believe the "melting ice cube" narrative prevails.

This is reinforced as the top and bottom line appear to be in structural decline, as depicted in the CAGR table below.

SIRI's ~15% float shorted, according to Yahoo Finance, also highlights the market's dissatisfaction with practically zero growth, and the expectations that this will continue in this way for at least the next five years.

But quite frankly, little of this is new and tends to be the main driver of the thesis. What really matters for SIRI's thesis is free cash flow, deleveraging, and cash return. The management team has candidly spoken that in FY26 the expectation is for fewer subscribers, relatively stable revenues (in this case stagnant), EBITDA ok (margins ranging at 31.5%), but FCF growing. Growing FCF without top- and bottom-line growth involves financial engineering. Sirius XM's main driver today is structural cost-cutting—considering $250 million in savings already delivered and another $100 million forecasted—and a migration to an ROI-based expensing model, i.e., less inefficient marketing and less spending on streaming that doesn't convert.

The end of the heavy CapEx cycle, with the recent satellites already launched, also creates room for FCF growth continuation.

Thus, it was possible to deliver $1.25 billion in FCF in FY25, which is a 24% YoY growth at the same time that revenues and adj. EBITDA declined by 2%. And the management team's expectation is for FCF to reach $1.35 billion in FY26, growing ~8% YoY.

This means that, when we look at Sirius XM over the last five years through a cash flow and capital allocation lens, the company has achieved the best of both worlds: deleveraging (~30% net deleveraging) without sacrificing returns (more than half of the FCF for shareholders).

Sirius XM and iHeartMedia and the Growth Trade-Off

The potential merger with iHeartMedia alone contradicts SIRI's current thesis. What has driven Sirius XM's ~22.8% gain over the past twelve months, even with widespread market skepticism, is largely driven by its predictable FCF and capital discipline.

An eventual deal would immediately point to a change in direction and use of capital—something non-trivial, and that explains the immediate 5% drop with speculated M&A.

While this creates "unnecessary" complexity for the equity thesis as a "yield story," an eventual M&A reveals something deeper. That is, Sirius XM is trying to solve its core problem: growth. iHeartMedia is expected to grow revenue by ~7% in FY26, which already shows a very different growth profile from Sirius XM.

For context, there is nothing concrete about the M&A, just a Bloomberg news report that says that "iHeartMedia holds talks about a possible sale to Sirius XM." The most likely scenario is stock, debt, and a complex structure. In this case, SIRI, as an acquirer, would absorb or combine with IHRT, and a possible stock swap and debt restructuring. This could be a significant problem. iHeartMedia is highly leveraged as it has $4.5 billion in net debt and a debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 6.6x.

Worse, IHRT's top and bottom line growth has been practically zero in the last three years, which would combine two structurally declining businesses.

And although iHeartMedia also has a strong cash generation profile, having generated ~$2.29 billion in FCF between 2021 and 2025, of this amount, zero of it returned to shareholders, ~42% went to real deleveraging, and the rest all went to interest. This makes me label iHeartMedia's thesis as an equity subordinated to debt.

Why the M&A Is Likely Not Great for Sirius XM

Based on the guidance provided by iHeartMedia for FY26, the company expects to generate ~$800 million in adjusted EBITDA and ~$200 million in free cash flow. This would be a welcome addition to Sirius XM's cash generation, but at the expense of having to deal with a balance sheet with ~$14.3 billion in net debt. Considering the addition of the FY26 guidance of both companies, which would imply $3.4 billion of adjusted EBITDA, this would mean a leverage ratio of ~4.2x.

Not that this leverage level is necessarily absurd for leveraged media plays, but it is certainly more stretched considering that part of this adj. EBITDA will come from a more volatile business like iHeartMedia.

It is worth mentioning that Sirius XM has consistently been setting a leverage target in the low- to mid-3 range, with real goals to achieve this by the end of this year. In the last earnings call, when asked if it was the intention to reach this level as soon as possible, CEO Jennifer Witz responded by saying that there is money to be deployed, but it will not rush into it.

End of year is right around the corner, and we have line of sight to that. So we're very comfortable that we'll get to that target range. And then again, $1.5 billion of target free cash flow next year. And if you think about the dividend as being, I think, around $350 million, that's a lot of excess cash to deploy. And of course, we want to focus on investments in the business that have high ROI. We don't see any near-term M&A opportunities, but that would be an option. And then otherwise, it's more capital returns for shareholders. And whether that's dividends or share repurchases, I think we'll have more opportunity to talk about that going forward.

The CEO's comments seemed much more focused on a buyback setup, capital discipline, and gradual deleveraging than a large M&A deal, which could push leverage far above the target that has been widely discussed in recent quarters.

My biggest skepticism for this M&A to happen is that buying iHeartMedia will very possibly not solve the problem of Sirius XM, which is a lack of organic growth. iHeartMedia has grown poorly, has more potential to grow than SIRI, but it is certainly far from a solid growth story. If it becomes an audio platform involving radio, satellite, and podcasts, and this results in real synergy gains, then the combined FCF could grow meaningfully. But I wouldn't have that as a base scenario.

So I believe this would go along the lines of "add a problem to a problem" for Sirius XM's thesis. The market would not forgive bad execution in any way if the deal were to materialize.

Final Remarks

That being said, Sirius XM is set to report its Q1 results on April 30 during market hours, and this tends to be a very modest quarter in top-line growth, with expectations of revenues reaching $2.07 billion (+0.24%) and EPS at $0.71 (+5%).

If the quarter has all the clues that there will be nothing exciting on the surface, I believe that the most interesting thing for investors will be monitoring FCF trajectory, progress in cost savings, and churn/subs. Even if revenues remain flat, I believe that the thesis would only break if cash flow deteriorated.

The recent speculation with iHeartMedia adds a layer of uncertainty that, at the same time, also reinforces the underlying tension in SIRI's story. If the eventual M&A brings optionality to the thesis around growth, it will most likely have a real cost for the positioning that currently supports the thesis: resilient FCF and capital returns.

If I were a SIRI shareholder, I would not want to change the profile from "equity as a yield story" to something hybrid with a questionable optionality of a business that is unlikely to grow materially to compensate for the risk it would bring to Sirius XM's balance sheet. The bottom line is that I would wait for the dust to settle before making any move. Based on how it is being speculated, the M&A looks more negative than beneficial, and so I’m sticking with a Hold rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Tesla: Playing Robo-Taxi 'Catch-Up'
data.description:
Cybercab finally going into production

Tesla (NASDAQ: TSLA) released their Q1 numbers to positive fanfare that quickly waned with Tesla now flat to slightly down for the month:

The main positive talking point in the earnings release was the start of mass production for the Cybercab:

I'v been rather vocal in prior articles about my opinions on the autonomous taxi space. I've been impressed by Google's (GOOGL) Waymo offerings traversing possibly the most complicated streets in the world in San Francisco. I took one of Amazon's (AMZN) ZOOX up and down the Las Vegas strip. I've yet to spend time in Austin Texas, but I would have assumed by how much Tesla hyped the robo-taxi business as their future revenue and earnings growth driver that I would be seeing them absolutely everywhere. It's quite the opposite, I see Tesla pretty far behind and in catch-up mode.

In Las Vegas for instance, I found it difficult to comprehend that Tesla actually bore a tunnel under the Las Vegas strip to make a one way un-impeded trip for riders. No traffic, no Las Vegas tourists to avoid, just it's own lane. ZOOX was dodging drunken party goers, getting cut off by Uber drivers at the Wynn and I still didn't witness a single odd incident.

ZOOX of course like Waymo is covered in Lidar detectors, something that Tesla opted not to do in order to cut down on costs. It seems to be taking longer to train the Tesla FSD systems to the point they can safely be on the road in mass.

Q1 Numbers

The company did have better numbers than expected and year over year revenue growth over Q1 2025. That's not saying much in my opinion, if we harken back to the beginning of 2025, CEO Elon Musk was heading up the DOGE and irking large parts of his customer base. That seems to have worn off a bit, time heals all wounds. The question still remains what is the future TAM if China is now dominating not only their own market with EVs, but also Europe and soon to be Canada?

Deliveries were up 6% in total year over year for same quarter previous year, but still fell over the previous two quarters. In my opinion especially for deliveries, eying the trailing quarters is more important than the year over year trend. Model 3 still drives the automotive sales and had tepid growth same quarter previous year with falling numbers over the trailing 2 quarters.

We see the same issues in revenue and Adjusted EBITDA. Better year over year for same quarter previous year, but falling compared to the trailing quarters. Q1 2025 was a low bar to beat and not a great indicator.

Tesla does not trade on fundamentals

With all the above being said about revenue and earnings growth, Tesla is still not a fundamental story. It is a bet on the CEO brining a futuristic fantasy into reality. As long as new and exciting stories are told, the investor base will ignore numbers.

Even when using the most liberal of earnings metrics, EV/EBITDA with non-cash and interest expense add-backs, trading at over 119 X is even expensive when comparing Tesla to itself. Price has gone really nowhere since 2022 yet the numbers have gotten worse in conjunction. Growth has stalled and earnings multiples have risen in turn.

2022-early 23 is when Tesla detached from reality

Tesla was a growth story that even I was intrigued with at one point. We can see the purple line [price] following upward revenue and earnings growth that promptly receded when rates began to hike in 2022. Price however did not retreat and remains elevated compared to fundamentals.

Balance sheet

The balance sheet still remains a key fundamental advantage of Tesla over the rest of the automotive industry. The company can continue to float new shares to both raise cash and pay stock based compensation. Share float and cash trace each other [orange and blue lines].

Balance sheet item Amount in Billions
Long-Term Debt $13.77B
Cash & Investments $44.45B
Net Debt -$30.68B
EBITDA $11.00B
Net Debt / EBITDA NET CASH POSITION

As we can see, Tesla has a net cash position and very little long term debt. As long as Tesla is considered a 'tech' company rather than auto, this will continue to be a reason to stay in the stock for long term holders. This buys time to try to uncover that next growth driver.

What I'm optimistic about

I'm not all negative on Tesla, but at this point with growth stalling out, I also can't confidently build any price target models that would say "I'm paying X times 2030 at a Y growth rate".

Semitruck

Tesla is beginning the 'pilot' production of the Tesla Semi at Giga 1 Nevada. This is the niche I see Tesla having a key advantage over competition. The total addressable market in autonomous freight lines would be massive if it works in my opinion. I would be very excited as a Tesla shareholder if I began to see actual autonomous freight delivery. If all it ends up being is a gas alternative with a driver on board, I don't see where this creates an efficiency for semi operators to buy in.

Interest rates

It's quite obvious to me at least that fundamentals started to shrink as interest rates began to rise. Short term rates falling effect auto loans more immediately than home loans [being attached to shorter durations]. If and when new Fed Chair Kevin Warsh gets confirmed, many are anticipating short term rates to fall fairly fast. If so, that should be an immediate boost to all auto companies including Tesla as these are products typically paid for with debt.

Optimus

The report also noted that Optimus production will begin taking over the Fremont factory by Q2 2026. I'll believe it when I see it but more robots in the mainstream will also be a plus for Tesla. This seems to be another point Tesla hyped but is falling behind in. Chinese robots and other US brands are hitting influencer channels on YouTube,TikTok and X daily. Optimus is still confined to Tesla factory demos while others are out in the real world.

Summary

I believe Tesla is a hold. The balance sheet, possible falling auto loan rates and the roll out of the cybercab finally coming to fruition are worthwhile catalysts to hold on rather than sell. I don't however see any fundamental strength that would warrant a buy rating at this moment. Investors also need to brace themselves for the fact that Tesla technology is no longer leaps and bounds ahead of competition. There is ample comparable, and in many instances better, tech out there in many areas Tesla competes.

Tesla needs to get back to providing unique features that do not yet exist. The Semi truck currently is the only segment I see where Tesla has no competition. They should attack that in my opinion.

data.title: Kimball Electronics: Contract Manufacturer Showing Signs Of Life
data.description:
Shares of Kimball Electronics (KE) have been lagging a bit in recent times, unlike the rest of the market, and certainly the segment of the market related to AI. In September of last year, I believed that the stock was getting electrified, while I was not. Ever since, shares are down minimally, while the wider market has risen some 10% here.

Shares have been lagging, while the operating business has shown signs of life, notably in terms of margins and growing medical sales, all to be applauded. This makes me slightly more upbeat here from a risk-reward perspective, but I'm not yet sold on the idea of getting involved at current levels.

Other interesting corporate events, including M&A stories, are discussed more extensively at Value In Corporate Events.

A Soft Performer

Early in February, Kimball Electronics reported a softer second-quarter earnings report. Sales were down 5% to $341 million, driven by a 13% fall in automotive sales to $162 million, with these sales falling just below half of total sales. Medical sales rose some 15% to $96 million, as the remaining industrial segment saw sales fall 5% to $83 million.

So while topline sales were soft, driven by automotive, margins were actually doing very well. Adjusted operating margins rose by no less than 80 basis points to 4.5% of sales. This was driven by a 160 basis point improvement in gross margins to 8% and change.

In between all this, adjusted earnings of $0.28 per share were down a penny on the year before, with adjusted earnings in the first half of the year reported at $0.77 per share. These are up twenty-six cents on the year before, supported by a stronger first quarter. However, stock-based compensation expenses rose twelve cents for the six-month period, as otherwise earnings are seen around $0.65 per share.

Despite a decline in sales, the company hiked the midpoint of the full-year sales guidance to $1.43 billion, a thirty million improvement on the previous guidance. Adjusted operating income is seen between 4.2% and 4.5% of sales, suggesting adjusted operating income around $62 million. This suggests more of the same for the second half of the year, after these earnings came in at $32 million and changed in the first half of the year.

Net debt is reported at $76 million, which should be manageable here. Nonetheless, all this is not too inspiring; after 2025, sales came in at $1.49 billion, with these revenues down substantially from the year before. Adjusted operating profits of $61 million were reported in 2025, when adjusted earnings were reported at $1.12 per share, with realistic earnings seen just below a dollar per share. As a result, more of the same in terms of adjusted earnings is seen in 2026.

Where Are We Coming From?

Kimball Electronics has a solid long-term track record, which has been impacted a bit in recent times. A decade ago, this was a roughly $900 million business, one that doubled sales to $1.8 billion in 2023, with sales down about 20% ever since. Historically, margins have come in at low to mid-single-digit operating margins, which is pretty common for contract manufacturing, with about 15% of the shares bought back over the past decade.

Shares have been trading in a $10-$30 range over the past decade, and despite the lackluster results in the last two years, shares have moved up to the higher end of the range, pushing up expectations. Earnings are seen close to a dollar per share, which looks demanding given a moderate debt load and a $27 current share price.

That is quite demanding after the company called for a second year in which earnings and sales were declining. Investors are upbeat on these shares, just like its peers. However, such peers actually show great operating benefits from the current boom, actually demonstrating strong sales and earnings growth.

Improved Positioning

While the topline numbers do not show strong results, the company is undergoing a real positioning towards the medical vertical, which grew sales some 15% on an annual basis. These, however, make up just over a quarter of sales.

While the company is looking to return to break-even growth results in sales in the second half of the year, and benefit from margin gains, earnings might improve from here going forward. That could be a real driver yet, while the margin and medical sale momentum are applauded, overall results show continued softness here.

This makes me a bit cautious, as investors really have to believe in management executing here. For now I am working with realistic earnings of around a dollar per share. Such earnings power and modest net debt load do not automatically create a compelling outlook at $27 per share.

However, I like the fact that shares have come in a little, while some real medical and margin green shoots are seen here. This is to be applauded greatly here.

A Final Word

In between shares of Kimball having lagged wider markets by about 15% over the past half a year or so, appeal has been improving here. Sluggish share price momentum and better operating performance are two welcomed developments taking place, both going in the right direction.

This makes that appeal is improving here, as a roughly 30 times earnings multiple in September has come down a bit to about 25 times here, while some green shoots are seen.

Nonetheless, valuations are not cheap enough to have conviction here, although I like the direction of travel of the business, its performance, and slightly lower valuations. However, these are still too demanding valuations to get involved here.

data.title: Hasbro: Magic Is Working, But I'm Not Spellbound
data.description:
Thesis

Hasbro, Inc. (HAS), the nearly century-old entertainment and toy giant behind brands like Monopoly, Magic: The Gathering, and Transformers, has been on my radar for close to a decade now. I first started covering Hasbro, Inc. in 2016 and gave it a Buy rating. My view was based on the company’s big plan to move beyond just making toys and become a broader entertainment and storytelling company (for example, creating content around its brands).

The call returned ~63% (total return since the rating), but compared with the S&P 500’s (SP500) ~230% gain over the same period, it underperformed. Fast forward to 2024, with digital gaming gains offset by deteriorating traditional toy sales, high debt, and thin revenue growth, and I downgraded to Hold. Recently, the stock has had a volatile few weeks, first dipping after Hasbro confirmed it's walking away from an Indian toy supplier, then snapping back on strong Q1 guidance.

Today, after breaking down what’s actually changed and whether this warrants an upgrade, I’ve decided to keep my neutral rating in place, as Hasbro’s recent strength looks more like execution on expectations than the start of a meaningfully stronger long-term growth story.

Hasbro’s Momentum Is About to Be Tested

  • Revenue: $1.45B
  • Revenue growth: +31.25% Y/Y.

  • EPS: $1.51
  • Adjusted operating profit: $315M
  • Adjusted operating margin: 21.8%.

FY 2025

  • Revenue: $4.7B
  • Revenue growth: +14%
  • Adjusted operating profit: $1.1B
  • Adjusted operating margin: 24.2%
  • Operating cash flow: $893M.

Wizards of the Coast/MAGIC

  • Q4 Wizards revenue: $630M (+86%)
  • FY Wizards revenue: $2.2B (+45%)
  • FY MAGIC revenue growth: ~60%
  • Q4 MAGIC growth: +141%.

Hasbro finished 2025 on a strong note, and its early Q1 2026 results suggest that momentum is continuing. Sales exceeded Wall Street expectations, profits increased, and the company confirmed its full-year targets even after experiencing a hacker attack and supply chain issues. One of the main reasons all of this is working is Magic: The Gathering. The Avatar: The Last Airbender and Final Fantasy card sets were a bonus, and Wizards of the Coast had the best quarter in its history, but again, Magic is carrying much of the company’s success. When a company sells a lot more, profits don’t always rise just as fast, because higher sales can also come with higher costs. However, in this case, Hasbro has been able to sell more premium Magic products, which increase profit margins compared with the small, inexpensive plastic toys it would normally sell.

Tariffs/Supply Chain

  • FY tariff impact: ~$70M
  • 2026 tariff cost estimate: ~$60M
  • 2026 cost savings target: ~$150M.

With respect to tariffs, Hasbro's CFO, Gina Goetter, noted that approximately 60–65% of the company’s total tariff costs for the year came during Q4. Even so, because of efficiencies gained within its supply chain, the company was able to offset the impact of those tariffs rather than have it flow through to the bottom line. The company is moving its manufacturing operations out of China to reduce its tariff exposure and is targeting 30% of toy and game sourcing from China by 2026, as tariffs are expected to worsen, not improve. Good plan. Not easy to pull off.

Which brings us to Aequs. Hasbro is dumping Aequs as a supplier. The official reason given by Hasbro for this move is that it is simply supply chain cleanup and is not related to the hacking incident on March 28. Perhaps so. But switching suppliers during times like these always brings challenges. A new factory partner takes time to get up to speed, and shipment risks are higher than with the current supplier. Plus, it's still unclear whether the original contract with Aequs remains active and what the ongoing discussions regarding it will lead to. This could also introduce additional complications with the switch to the new supplier. The real issue to watch will be whether Hasbro's toy and game shipments in the second quarter of 2026 face delays due to both the fallout from the hacking and the supplier switch happening at the same time.

Q1 2026 Preliminary

  • Revenue: $970M–$985M
  • Revenue growth: +9% to +11% Y/Y
  • Consensus estimate: $909M
  • MAGIC shipments remained on schedule.

Hasbro has handled its public communication on the hacking about as well as could be expected. The company stated that it appeared to be contained, its Q1 financials were unaffected, and Magic card shipments, including the April launch of Secrets of Strixhaven, were still on schedule. Morgan Stanley indicated that Q1 revenue was approximately 10% higher than the same period last year, right in the middle of Hasbro's guidance range, while analyst expectations were for only 3% growth. Operating profit also significantly outperformed forecasts. Once again, it was Wizards of the Coast.

2026 Full-Year Guidance

  • Revenue growth: +3% to +5% constant currency
  • Adjusted operating margin: 24%–25%
  • Adjusted EBITDA: $1.40B–$1.45B
  • Share repurchase authorization: $1B.

Hasbro's management maintained its full-year targets of constant-currency revenue growth, an adjusted operating margin of 24%–25%, and its EBITDA target. That tells us management believes it can see what's coming, at least on the Magic and Disney entertainment side. Overall, Hasbro is expecting to recover some of the lost sales due to the cyberattack in the second half of 2026, with that recovery tied to delayed toy and game shipments being made up. If consumers pull back, it gets dicey. CEO Chris Cocks said on the Q4 earnings call that he’s closely tracking the widening divide between higher- and lower-income households, noting that the two groups are spending in markedly different ways right now.

Hasbro’s Upside Looks Limited From Here

FAST Graphs shows the stock trading at a blended P/E of 16.91x, fairly close in line with its normal P/E of 17.23x, but above the 15.00x fair value ratio. That puts fair value at ~$84, meaning, from this angle, I'm thinking the stock already reflects a lot of the Wizards and digital gaming recovery. Meanwhile, the estimated annual return of 5.14% isn't exactly poor, especially with a 2.94% dividend yield, but it's not compelling enough to ignore that balance sheet. Long-term debt to capital is still pretty high at 72.94%, the credit rating is BBB, and the total EV of $16.18 billion is above the $13.38 billion market cap.

Moving over to Seeking Alpha’s valuation table for more context, it seems to be pointing to the same tension. The forward non-GAAP P/E of 16.49x is only modestly above the sector, but EV/Sales at 3.23x and Price/Sales at 2.72x are more than double sector medians. Price/book is very stretched at 14.13x forward.

I think growth helps justify some of that premium, with forward revenue growth of 8.23%, forward EBIT growth of 15.62%, and forward EPS growth of 16.38%.

But keep in mind, the long-term EPS growth estimate is only 5.90%, below the sector, and levered free cash flow growth is negative on a trailing basis.

Technically, HAS is just above its 10-day and 50-day averages and 13.24% above the 200-day, while RSI at 53.67 signals neither panic nor euphoria.

The forward P/E ratio is expected to fall to about 15x earnings by 2028, which is roughly what many investors would consider fair valuation at today’s price. The stock seems to reflect expectations of strong execution and solid results over a multi-year stretch, with limited tolerance for surprises. Even so, failure to meet anticipated performance in either the Consumer Products sector or the Magic cadence during Q2 will severely undermine this hypothesis.

Final Takeaway

I'm going to hold my shares and recommend the same. HAS isn't a high-flyer, but I like owning this icon in my portfolio. Keep in mind, the majority of my research here is long-term oriented. Only a small slice is based on high-risk quick trades. That said, most of Hasbro’s "Magic-led" earnings momentum appears priced in near current levels. Any income-oriented holders out there can stay involved for the dividend and improving margins and the opportunity to auto-reinvest. As for new buyers kicking the tires, for the most part, you could nibble into this with a small starter position. But I'd wait for a more attractive entry point or stronger operational proof that would improve the setup.

data.title: Realty Income: Improving Real Returns
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Realty Income, Inc. (NYSE:O) has produced modest returns over the last 10 years. In nominal dollars, the share price has averaged $61 while oscillating significantly. Dividends dominate Total Returns. Alpha vs. a real estate index is tiny.

In real dollars the results are more stark; the share price is down almost a third, the dividend is flat, and total returns are only about 3% a year.

These results reflect a buy-and-hold strategy, usually the implicit default assumption, particularly for retirement investors, who often anticipate holding periods of 10 to 20 years.

An alternative strategy that anticipates share price oscillation - i.e. with disciplined entry/exit points - may produce improved results.

Realty Income Overview

The information in this article is primarily drawn from the 2025 Annual Report. The Q4 2025 Earnings Call Transcript (25 February 2026) and the Global Property Conference Transcript (03 March 2026). Unless otherwise stated, the numbers in the Business description section below reflect year-end 2025. Market share numbers are based on Annualized Base Rent (ABR).

This article will generally use a 10-year analysis period. This is long enough to support a strategic assessment while also reflecting the decreasing relevance of more distant historical performance, as the company, the management, and the market environment have materially changed.

There are some minor differences in historical data between Seeking Alpha, YCharts, and other sources, but these discrepancies do not appear to be material to the analysis.

In a March 2025 article, Realty Income: Unjustified Enthusiasm, I wrote that the previous decade's data "suggests that while O will probably provide modest gains, attractive returns will demand opportunistic, rather than automatic, investment."

We will look at O's individual and relative performance in nominal dollars, performance in real terms, examine an alternative to buy and hold, and discuss several issues and risks to consider in assessing O's potential future performance.

The Business

O is the 7th largest REIT by market cap (per Seeking Alpha), with 16 thousand properties. Marketed as The Monthly Dividend Company®, with a declared objective to deliver dependable monthly dividends, O enjoys iconic status with many retail investors.

While historically and still primarily focused on owning standalone retail properties in the United States, rented under net lease terms, it has diversified by property type, geography, and ownership structure.

Industrial (15%) and gambling (3%) properties have been added.

About 19% of annual rent comes from 8 countries in Europe (primarily the U.K.), and about 60% of investment in 2025 was directed to Europe. In January 2026, the footprint was extended to Mexico.

Expanding beyond traditional equity ownership, by the end of 2025, Realty Income held about $3.1 billion in loans and preferred equity interest. Private capital funds (where Realty Income holds a less than 100% stake and earns a management fee) have been introduced. Major deals in 2026 include an investment by the Singapore sovereign wealth fund, GIC, to fund a joint venture fund to develop build-to-suit industrial properties in Mexico, and a $1 billion investment by Apollo Global Management (APO) to acquire a 49% interest in a joint venture portfolio of 500 current net lease properties, to be managed by O.

In 2025, Realty Income evaluated about $120 billion in potential investments (55% US, 45% Europe) and actually invested $6 billion, i.e., a 5% rate for buy vs. assess. They believe their organization can support a $10-12 billion per year buy rate.

About 32% of annual revenue comes from investment-grade clients, and about 35% of revenue comes from their 20 largest clients. Grocery (11%) and convenience stores (10%) are the two largest concentrations of revenue by industry.

In 2025, AFFO per share was $4.28, rent recapture was 103.9%, $6.2 billion was invested, 7.3% initial cash yield, 425 properties were sold for $744 million. The portfolio had contractual ABR increases of about 1.5% CAGR. O had a 5.4x net debt to adjusted EBITDA, an annualized run rate of $900 million in free cash flow, and 3.2% cash G&A. Headcount increased 16% in 2025, to 554.

For 2026, O estimates AFFO per share of $4.38 to $4.42 (up 2.8%), and $8 billion in investments (about 3:1 public vs. private capital), with dispositions similar to 2025. O expects to generate approximately $10 million of base management fees from private funds (with about 70% margins).

O enjoys an A- credit rating from S&P.

Realty Income Performance

We will begin by looking at performance over the past decade, assuming a buy-and-hold strategy.

Share Price

The two charts below show the nominal share price. The average share price for the decade is about $61, with multiple excursions above and below this average value. In my view, these two charts alone should make investors cautious about automatic investment strategies.

Currently the share price is up about 10% for the decade, but that can vary significantly, depending on the specific period selected for analysis.

By eye, most of the variance falls within +- 15% of the average, i.e., in a range of about $52-$70.

Dividends

Dividends are an important part of O's return and are paid monthly. The nominal dividend is up 40% over 10 years.

As might be expected given the roughly flat stock price, the resulting yield has grown over the decade by about 31%.

The average yield is 4.37% over the decade (shown in the chart below) but has been increasing in recent years; 4.58% over five years, 4.79% over three years, and 5.07% over one year.

Total Return

Most investors value Total Return, reflecting the impact of both share price and dividends. The chart below includes both Total Return and Share Price, which highlights that the bulk – roughly 85% – of the Total Return over the last decade comes from dividends rather than share price appreciation. This is particularly evident in the most recent years.

Real Returns

Thus far, we have been considering nominal returns, i.e., ignoring inflation. However, investors buy future groceries and gasoline with real dollars.

In nominal terms, over the decade the share price has increased about 10%, the dividend about 40%, and the Total Return has been about 76%.

Inflation over the last 10 years has been about 39%, per the inflation calculator provided by the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics.

Thus, in real terms, considering inflation, over the last decade the real share price is down about 29%, the dividend is up about 1%, and the total return is about 37%.

Note that this assumes reinvestment of the dividend and no losses to taxes. Under that assumption, one might assess O as a reasonable store of value. However, if an investor is spending the dividends on monthly bills, they would now have a significant real capital loss and a dividend payout that has kept up with inflation.

An investor might ask if there are better options. To try to answer that question, we will first consider how O looks in comparison to some other investments and then take a look at an alternative to a buy-and-hold strategy.

Realty Income's Relative Performance

Let's first take a look at O's Total Return performance vs. other large REITs. The comparison group here are the six REITs with larger market caps, plus the Vanguard Real Estate Index ETF (VNQ), which provides a broad real estate market benchmark. (Note we are back to nominal dollars here).

All of these names are exposed to the same fluctuations in general economic conditions, such as interest rates, but may reflect sector-specific issues, e.g., supply and demand in the sector. In hindsight, O was not the best choice for this decade.

The correlation between O and VNQ is very notable and is more clearly evident in the plot below. O demonstrated almost no Alpha advantage vs. the index, which frankly surprised me.

O is still primarily a triple net retail REIT, although it has a material industrial component (15% of ABR) and is branching out into other sectors. If we look at O and three other (and significantly smaller) REITs focused on the retail triple net sector, we get the chart below. O doesn't win this intra-sector contest.

Finally, we recall many investors' comments to the effect that O is now a "bond equivalent". We can look at some data below, also keeping in mind that if we adopt an alternative to buy and hold, we will also need a place to park the money during periods when we are not holding O.

We look below at two well-regarded bond funds, a high-yield corporate bond fund and a short-term corporate bond fund, but there are many others.

The Total Return from the high-yield fund is similar to O over the decade, but with much less variation. The short-term bond is, as one might expect, even steadier but provides less than half the Total Return.

If we look at the yield differences, shown in the two charts below, the high-yield bond fund averages about 1.1% higher yield than O, and the short-term bond about 1.7% less than O.

If we focus in on just the last three years (which omits the extreme COVID-era variations and ultra-low rate period), we see the yield from O falling between the yields from the bond funds. A mix of the two bond funds would have generally matched O's yield with more stability.

Valuation

Seeking Alpha reports the Wall Street consensus view is that O is slightly below fair value today, i.e., about 8% below the average 12-month forward estimates of $68, with a range of $62 to $75. The analyst's average rating has been fairly steadily declining for about 2.5 years, from 3.9 to 3.4, with 16 of 24 analysts currently rating it a Hold.

One may view the tone and focus of analyst's questions in the last earnings call as very politely probing O's plans and ability to grow materially. From one analyst:

I wanted to go back to your comments regarding the other investment avenues maturing and getting back to a more historical level of growth in 3 to 5 years, especially considering many investors are not necessarily looking for a long-term wait-and-see story.

Seeking Alpha's summary Valuation rating is C+.

In my view, O is probably about fairly valued at its current price. It's notable how close this is to the $61 average for the last decade.

Alternative Strategy: Disciplined Buy/Sell

Let's look at an alternative to the default buy-and-hold strategy, specifically a disciplined buy/sell strategy based on nominal share price.

Nominal Share Price - Buy at $55, Sell at $65

The first step is to look at the data on O's price variance. Initial inspection of share price plots may be misleading, as the curves are both sometime smoothed and difficult to read in detail. Fortunately, Seeking Alpha offers share price data in tabular format, with several temporal granularities. I pulled the monthly high and low data from January 2016 to February 2026 and plotted it in the graph below.

As we noted in the earlier Y-Chart of price data, the decade average price is about $61. The average intra-monthly high-low price delta is $4.56. By inspection of the monthly data points in the table and graphic, a "Buy at $55, Sell at $65" strategy looks reasonable. Note that this strategy generates an 18% capital gain per buy/sell cycle.

Reviewing the tabular data makes it easy to identify the timing of the buy and sell events, which are summarized below. I've also added the duration of the holding periods with this model.

This strategy would have signaled 5 Buy-Sell cycles over the past 10 years, leaving the investor holding O about half the time and an alternative asset half the time. With this model, an investor would currently be out of O.

The 5 buy/sell cycles at 18% would have yielded a 91% capital gain. Assuming the cash was put into a mix of bond funds when not holding O, one might conservatively estimate an additional 40% return from dividends. That would give a rough back-of-the-envelope Total Return for the decade of about 130%, approaching twice the 76% actually observed.

This nominal price point strategy might be refined. One drawback is that it ignores the influence of inflation. For instance, if we started with buy/sell points at $55/$65 and adjusted for CPI over the last decade, we now get points at $76/$90. Limiting the adjustment to $1 a year (i.e., about 1.5%) would generate current buy/sell points at $65/$75.

Basing buy/sell points on dividend yield might also offer advantages.

Some Observations

I'd like to make some observations here based on the data we looked at earlier and two more general observations relevant to O's plans to increase their growth rate.

Performance

  • O's nominal share price has on average been remarkably flat, albeit with +-15% excursions.
  • O significantly underperformed five of six other larger REITs. It also underperformed significantly smaller REITs in the retail triple net sector.
  • O's Total Return was highly correlated with, and only slightly exceeded, the real estate index.
  • O roughly matched high-yield bond fund performance, due to weak O share price performance.
  • High-yield and/or short-term corporate bond funds might reasonably serve as a cash-holding mechanism if an investor is temporarily out of O.
  • The dividend penalty for being out of O is probably 2% or less and could be quite small with a split between high-yield and corporate bond funds.
  • An alternative strategy, disciplined buy/sell at nominal share price points, would have produced almost twice the Total Return delivered by buy and hold.

European Expansion Carries Risks

GDP per person in almost every country in Europe, including the UK, is lower than Mississippi, the poorest state in the US, according to this analysis. Projections by the International Monetary Fund suggest GDP growth for the EU will be lower than for the US.
