Among other factors, energy costs are substantially higher in Europe (~2x to 4x US prices), particularly for industrial users.

Management Fees May Not Be Material

O has talked about the advantages of the Spread + Fee model, i.e., earning a fee by managing an investment 80% funded with external capital. In the last earnings call, Jonathan Pong, Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer, responded to a question:

let's just assume, for round numbers, we're getting about 1 point from the 80% of equity we're managing on someone else's behalf

They expect to earn about $10 million in such fees in 2026, with a roughly 70% margin after expenses. $10 billion under management would earn $100 million before costs. Assume costs are insignificant at that level. Spread across the current 932 million shares, that's about 11 cents a share. Against a $4.40 FFO/share baseline, that's a boost of about 2.5%. Not nothing, but not much.

Risks to Thesis

There are risks to the "buy at $55, sell at $65" thesis. I've listed a few below and suggested some mitigation actions.

1. O's average price may move permanently and materially higher, leaving the investor who sold at $65 with no opportunity to reenter at $55, or even $65.

This risk is mitigated to a degree by the temporary holding investment. There are also other fish in the sea.

2. With inflation at even 2-3%, the $55/$65 limits may become increasingly stale.

A reasonable mitigation option is to increase the limits by a dollar or two each year, i.e., about 1.5-3.0%.

3. The suggested $55/$65 limits might not be the best choices.

This is almost certainly true, and each investor who considers this strategy will have to decide that for themselves.

4. This strategy looks like market timing, the bane of many investors.

Yes. The willingness (and ability) to wait for the trade, perhaps for a couple of years, mitigates this at least to a degree. Patience is among the very few advantages available to individual investors. It also demands that investors pay some attention to O's price, which some may be unwilling or unable to do.

Investor Takeaway

A buy-and-hold strategy for Realty Income has not performed well over the last decade compared to other large REITs or other retail triple-net sector REITs. The current share price is still near the decade average of $61.

In real dollars, the Total Return over the decade has been modest; the share price is down by a third, and the dividend is flat.

An alternative strategy, disciplined buy/sell based on $55/$65 share price points, would have delivered nearly twice the Total Returns vs. a buy-and-hold strategy. Investors may wish to consider this or other alternatives.

Personally, I plan to try this strategy. I now have a limit order in place to sell 50% of my O shares at $65. My intent is to rebuy those shares around $55.

data.title: Harley-Davidson: Possibly The Next Big Short Squeeze
data.description:
Harley-Davidson, Inc. (HOG) is an iconic motorcycle manufacturer facing tariff impact, secular decline, an aging customer base, and declining market shares. The company's stock continues to be heavily shorted despite being undervalued—even for a declining company. We will now revisit HOG ahead of their Q1 earnings release and strategic plan presentation on May 5th. This article is an update to our previous article on the company.

Since our article, the stock has performed well, with a total return of over 17% in under 3 months, compared to an S&P 500 change of just under 3% over the same period. This move was supported by some notable insider purchases. However, the company is still trading well below our valuation target, and we still haven't seen large-scale short-covering.

Secular Decline

Looking back at the beautiful illustration of Harley-Davidson Unit Registration from a 2025 article by fellow Seeking Alpha contributor Caffital Research, the secular decline since the highs in the 2010s becomes clear. I also recommend checking out their latest article on HOG, providing valuable insights.

Based on updated data from the company's most recent 10-K, the trend continues with both registrations and market share declining compared to 2024.

The latest registration data shows a grim picture: the company has a declining market share in a declining market—both overall industry new motorcycle registrations declined, and their market share declined as well.

We covered the decline and financial metrics over the years in depth in our last article. The overall big picture remains unchanged: the company is still in secular decline. The decline is even accelerating based on the latest 10-K. In this article, however, we want to focus on the company's recently presented strategic plan and the continued short-selling of the stock.

Short-Selling

As extensively shown, Harley-Davidson is very visibly in long-term decline. This makes the company an extremely popular short-selling target for institutions and retail investors alike. Historically, the company has been a very profitable short target with a 5-year decline of over 52%.

The extreme pessimism surrounding the stock also becomes clear when looking at the comments on our previous article, broader social media negativity, or press coverage. Besides our “Strong Buy” rating early this year, there also hasn't been a single “Buy” or “Strong Buy” article on Seeking Alpha since June 2024. Mid-2024 is also around the last time Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating rated the stock a “Buy”; since then, it has been rated a “Hold” or “Sell” only.

HOG isn't a battleground stock—it's been a consensus short for years now.

While short-sellers definitely have been “right” so far, it looks like they have overdone it. As touched upon in our last article, we believe short-sellers have created somewhat of a self-fulfilling prophecy. This became obvious following the price increase from the strategic partnership with KKR and PIMCO in July 2025.

The relationship between stock price and short interest becomes even more evident when plotting share price snapshots with short interest snapshots over time.

There is a clear correlation: the share price is falling when net-short interest increases, and it's rising when net-short interest falls.

The most recent moves since we published our articles reveal a very interesting dynamic:

  1. Shortly after publishing our article at the end of January, the price fell to a bottom of under $18 in mid-March. In the same timeframe, net short interest increased from just 15.9 million shares on 15th January to over 18.3 million by mid-March. So short sellers added almost 2.5 million in net shorts in just around 2 months, correlating with a decline in price from just under $20 to under $18.
  2. Following the bottom in mid-March, the share price started recovering to almost $23 by mid-April. This correlates with a reduction in net short interest from over 18.3 million shares to around 16 million shares. So short sellers covered around 2.3 million in net shorts in a month, correlating with a rise in price from under $18 to just under $23.
  3. Cumulatively, shorts in this timeframe have been unprofitable. Net shorts were added at prices between around $18 and $20 and were covered at prices between $20 and $23. It also shows how covering 2.3m short shares led to a much stronger move than shorting 2.5m shares in the first place.

As the company is trading close to net financial assets and below any reasonable fair value estimates, short-sellers are fundamentally wrong. The relationship has now changed from fundamental to technical:

Short sellers stopped profiting from the declining stock price of a fundamentally weak company, and instead, they now start to cause technical weakness in the stock of a fundamentally undervalued company.

Once short sellers stop profiting from the decline and instead start causing it, they go from being structurally profitable to unprofitable. This is beautifully shown in the recent moves explained above.

Of course, this cannot continue indefinitely. At some point, short-sellers will have to cover their positions, whether to take profits or to cut losses. We don't think they'll be able to cover short interest at prices anywhere close to the current stock price, as shown in recent weeks, when just a small share of shorts were covered.

Additionally, the company has been aggressively buying back shares, putting further pressure on short-sellers. As a result of aggressive repurchases, short interest as a percentage of share float has been rising rapidly.

As can be seen in the charts above, short interest measured in shares is much lower than it was during the massive COVID-19 dip in 2020. When looking at short interest as a share of the float, however, short interest is close to its highest levels, at a massive 14.5% short interest as of 15th April 2026, around as high as the 15% peak-Covid short interest the stock had in 2020.

The pressure on short-sellers to cover their positions increases with time. We believe this all could either lead to a steady re-rating of the stock closer to our valuation target or even a violent short squeeze, causing the stock price to go even past our target.

In our opinion, the upcoming earnings release and strategic plan presentation could become a catalyst for an upside move. Following the release, we could also see further insider buying and share repurchases, putting even more pressure on short-sellers.

Valuation

The short-selling thesis for this company is extremely speculative and shouldn't be depended upon. As we believe the company is highly undervalued on a fundamental basis, we are confident even when ignoring short-selling theories.

Our valuation will become more refined once the company shares its strategic plan going forward. For now, however, we will stick to our valuation method from our last article.

As our last valuation, we will discount this adjusted value by 20%, resulting in an adjusted EV of around $900 million as of writing. Assuming 2026E FCF of around $300m at a 5x multiple, our target EV for the company is $1.5b. Adding the adjusted tangible book value of $1.6b leaves us with a $3.1b market cap for the company at our valuation target, or just under $30 per share at current shares outstanding. We will update our valuation based on the strategic plan.

Based on the extremely high short interest and continued repurchases at very attractive prices, we believe that the short-term potential for the stock could be even higher based on its short-squeeze potential.

The fact that the company is expected to continue to generate positive cash flow while already sitting on a big pile of cash emphasizes the current undervaluation and provides good context as to why we think our valuation is conservative.

Outlook

In the presentation, the company is planning to hold on 5th May, primarily because their strategic plan is important for investors. However, the date will also be used to release their Q1 2026 financial data.

Since the company, in their last earnings presentation, guided for a stabilization between sales and wholesale shipments, an important metric will be whether and how big any difference between those two numbers will be. A stated goal of the company is dealer health—making sure dealers sell more than they are forced to buy from the company is an important step to reach that goal.

Another important thing to look out for is the first numbers and commentary on how this year's new models perform. The first quarter is always especially important when it comes to sales of the new models.

As sequential financial numbers can be volatile based on shipment times, it's important to look out for any possible changes in guidance for the full year without reading too much into quarter-to-quarter metrics.

Besides the release of financial data from Q1 2026, we are even more excited about the company's strategic plan presentation. We are closely watching for any remarks regarding investments, cash burn, and plans to boost long-term demand for their motorcycles.

Risks and Conclusion

Even though the company is trading close to our adjusted tangible book values, there could still be some long-term downside for investors. Primarily, we see two main risks:

  1. Without a catalyst for re-rating, the stock could continue trading at or even below tangible book value for a long time, making opportunity risk a major concern for buyers.
  2. Investing in a turnaround or accelerated deterioration of fundamentals could lead to the company becoming cash-burning and destroying its tangible book value.

Investors should be able to better price both of these risks after digesting information from their upcoming strategic plan presentation.

The first risk could primarily be mitigated by the company continuing to push for aggressive share repurchases, forcing the stock to eventually trade at reasonable multiples above its tangible book value.

The second risk also depends on the company's plans going forward. A low-risk, low-reward scenario for investors certainly is managing the business for cash and continually right-sizing the company based on declining demand. A high-risk, high-reward scenario is the company investing heavily in a turnaround, spending money on new products and marketing.

If the company chooses to go down the high-risk path, there certainly is a substantial risk of the company investing its remaining capital in a failing turnaround, leaving the investors with even bigger losses.

data.title: Merck: Bullish Setup Into Q1 Earnings
data.description:
So, I am here again, with my analysis of Merck & Co., Inc. (MRK).

In my previous article, "Merck: Why Investors Should Remain Bullish Despite Patent Risks," I discussed why the subcutaneous version of pembrolizumab, called Keytruda Qlex, has a high chance of becoming Merck's next blockbuster, as well as the follow-up data from the Phase 2b KEYNOTE-942 trial, evaluating Moderna's (MRNA) intismeran autogene+Keytruda.

In this one, I'll focus on Merck's latest milestones, including its acquisition of Terns Pharmaceuticals (TERN) and the FDA approval of Idvynso on April 21, and, more importantly, what I expect from its Q1 earnings.

Terns' TERN-701 strengthens Merck's position in the chronic myeloid leukemia market

So, let me remind you that on March 25, it announced the acquisition of Terns for $5.7 billion.

And a month later, on April 24, it was informed about the end of the waiting period under the Hart-Scott-Rodino Act. Simply put, I believe Merck will close this deal before the end of May.

And in my view, TERN-701 is the "crown jewel" for which Merck paid billions of USD.

It is a highly selective allosteric inhibitor of the kinase activity of BCR-ABL1, specifically targeting the ABL1 myristoyl pocket.

Moreover, in preclinical studies, TERN-701 was more selective than Novartis' (NVS) Scemblix [asciminib, a first-in-class BCR-ABL1 STAMP inhibitor].

But still. Was this deal a smart strategic move for Merck?

I would say, yes.

First of all, on December 8 last year, Terns released updated results from the ongoing CARDINAL study [NCT06163430].

Namely, I want to talk about patients with chronic-phase CML [chronic myeloid leukemia] who were heavily pretreated with a median of 3 TKIs, and were taking TERN-701 at doses of 320 mg or 500 mg once daily.

So, in 30 efficacy-evaluable patients, the overall MMR [major molecular response] rate at 24 weeks was 80%. Moreover, at this recommended phase 2 dose of TERN-701, ≥ 320 mg QD, 36% of patients [10 out of 28] achieved deep molecular response [DMM].

And given these very strong numbers, coupled with its favorable safety profile, only strengthens my belief that TERN-701 will become the standard of care in 1L+ CML.

I said that for a reason.

In the Phase 3 ASCEMBL trial [NCT03106779], the major molecular response rate was only 25.5% in those taking asciminib at 24 weeks.

Also, the DMR rate was only 10.8% in the Scemblix arm.

But even these data, while weaker than those of TERN-701, are superior to other tyrosine kinase inhibitors, including Pfizer's (PFE) Bosulif and Bristol-Myers Squibb's (BMY) Sprycel.

And that's why, as I pointed out in the article "Novartis: Buy Thesis Fades Into Q1 2026 Results," sales of Scemblix grew 88.9% year-over-year to $391 million in Q4.

Thus, my first intermediate conclusion is that Merck was right to acquire Terns Pharmaceuticals.

I expect to see another solid performance from Keytruda and Winrevair

So, as you know, pembrolizumab is a PD-1 inhibitor and the flagship of Merck's oncology franchise.

In Q4, Keytruda+Keytruda Qlex generated about $8.37 billion in revenue for it, up 6.8% year-on-year.

Moreover, in the past 4 months, Merck has reached several milestones.

First of all, I highlight that the combo of Keytruda with Pfizer's Padcev reduced the risk of death by 35% versus neoadjuvant chemotherapy [P=0.0029] in cis-eligible patients with MIBC.

And also, Keytruda/Keytruda Qlex plus paclitaxel, with or without bevacizumab, got the EC's green light on April 2 for the treatment of certain patients suffering from platinum-resistant ovarian cancer.

To my great regret, WHO's Cancer Tomorrow doesn't provide numbers only for PD-L1-positive platinum-resistant ovarian cancer, but even without that, it's clear that the TAM is not so small, as about 45.8 thousand cases of ovarian cancer will be diagnosed only in the EU in 2050.

On the other hand, I was once again not disappointed with the "gem" of Merck's cardiovascular portfolio, Winrevair [sotatercept].

Alright. But why?

Winrevair net revenues rose 29.7% quarter-over-quarter to $467 million in Q4, in part due to its label expansion on October 27th last year.

And also, on March 29, in my view, Winrevair showed promising results in the Phase 2 CADENCE trial [NCT04945460].

In it, patients with Cpc-PH due to heart failure with preserved ejection fraction who took 0.3 mg/kg of sotatercept experienced a placebo-corrected reduction in pulmonary vascular resistance by 1.02 Wood units at week 24.

In my view, the results of the secondary endpoints further strengthen the evidence that Winrevair has direct cardiac and pulmonary vascular effects in these people with advanced HF.

And before I get to the "risks" section, I must note that under my "base case" scenario, the pembrolizumab franchise and Winrevair will generate $9.05 net revenues for Merck in Q1, up 20.9% YoY.

By the way, I also took into account that Roche's (RHHBY) Tecentriq, a key competitor of Keytruda in the PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint inhibitors market, once again showed weak performance.

In Q1 2026, its sales were only 811 million CHF, down 14.6% from last quarter.

Risks

While I have identified 7 risks that, in my view, could scare away income-oriented investors from adding Merck to their portfolios, the key one is the U.S. launch of generic versions of Janumet in May.

By the way, sales of this therapy for type 2 diabetes were $199 million in Q4, down 18.1% quarter-over-quarter and 22% YoY.

Takeaway

On April 30, Merck, one of the key players in the lung cancer and renal cell carcinoma therapeutics markets, will announce its first-quarter results.

According to SA’s data, Wall Street projects that its revenue will be $15.85 billion [from $15.23 billion to $16.32 billion; up 2% YoY] and non-GAAP EPS of -$1.47.

But personally, I'm a little bit more optimistic.

First of all, due to the dominant position of Merck’s Prevymis among the anti-CMV agents, Keytruda and its pneumococcal 21-valent vaccine called Capvaxive [its sales I expect to be $320 million vs. $279 million in Q4].

Well, my last remark is that I continue to cover Merck with a Buy rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
